REVIEW FOR MANDATORY SETTLEMENT CONFERENCE
DECLARATION OF STEVEN H. BOVARNICK IN OPPOSITION TO MOTION FOR ENFORCEMENT OF SETTLEMENT (TRANSACTION ID # 100075113) FILED BY PLAINTIFF WILLIAMS, TERESITA SANTOS

LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANTS' MOTION TO ENFORCE SETTLEMENT. EX PARTE APPLICATION TO SERVE AS MOVING PAPERS.DEFENDANTS ALBERTO A. SANTOS AND NELY SANTOS'S MOTION TO ENFORCE SETTLEMENT IS GRANTED. ON FEBRUARY 11, 2019, THE PARTIES ENTERED INTO A PARTLY HANDWRITTEN STIPULATION FOR SETTLEMENT, SIGNED BY ALL PARTIES AND COUNSEL, WHICH HAD BEEN PREPARED ON FEBRUARY 8, 2019 DURING MEDIATION WITH A NEUTRAL BENCH OFFICER AT ADR SERVICES. THE SIGNED STIPULATION FOR SETTLEMENT CONTAINS A PROVISION WHEREBY THE COURT RESERVED JURSIDICTION TO ENFORCE THE TERMS AND CONDITIONS OF THE SETTLEMENT PURSUANT TO CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE SECTION 664.6. THE STIPULATION FOR SETTLEMENT RECITED THAT IT WAS "INTENDED TO BE BINDING AND ENFORCEABLE" EFFECTIVE FEBRUARY 11, 2019 AND "REPRESENTS THE FINAL AGREEMENT BETWEEN THE PARTIES TO THIS DISPUTE." (SEE MINI-MINUTES FOR DETAILS) . COUNSEL AGREE TO CONFER REGARDING WITHDRAWAL OF LIS PENDENS. WRITTEN ORDER IS SIGNED AND FILED. JUDGE: ETHAN P. SCHULMAN; CLERK: J. RIOS MERIDA; REPORTER: ANTHONY VAUGHN, CSR NO. 6185, TONYVAUGHN@YAHOO.COM. =(302/EPS)

Defendants' Motion To Enforce Settlement. Ex Parte Application To Serve As Moving Papers.
Off Calendar Jun-24-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, Steven Howard Bovarnik of the law offices of Leland Parachini Steinberg et al LLP (415-957-1800) appears as counsel for plaintiff/opposing party

Attorney, David Butler of the law offices of David Butler (415-608-1214) appears as counsel for defendants/moving/moving parties, Aberto Santos and Nely Santos

The cause is argued and submitted by both sides in open court.

The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

Defendants' Motion To Enforce Settlement. Ex Parte Application To Serve As Moving Papers.

Defendants Alberto A. Santos and Nely Santos's motion to enforce settlement is granted. On February 11, 2019, the parties entered into a partly handwritten Stipulation for Settlement, signed by all parties and counsel, which had been prepared on February 8, 2019 during mediation with a neutral bench officer at ADR Services. The signed Stipulation for Settlement contains a provision whereby the court reserved jursidiction to enforce the terms and conditions of the settlement pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure Section 664.6. The Stipulation for Settlement recited that it was "intended to be binding and enforceable" effective February 11, 2019 and "represents the final agreement between the parties to this dispute."

Plaintiff Teresita Santos Williams states that "[s]oon after" signing the Stipulation for Settlement, she wanted to "rescind" the settlement on the ground that Defendant Alberto Santos purportedly told her at the courthouse "that [she] should sign the settlement because he did not want to go to trial and we would have a different settlement." (Williams Decl. 3, 7.) In a letter she sent to her attorney on May 31, 2019, she also asserted that at the time she signed the Stipulation for Settlement, she was "under stress, duress, confused, even coerced," and claimed that it "seems like I was misled, deceived and manipulated" by Santos. However, there are no facts before the Court to support Plaintiff's contention, conveyed solely to her attorney in a hearsay letter, that her consent was obtained by duress or fraud. Moreover, it is undisputed that the agreement was entered into after a mediation presided over by a retired judge and a settlement conference presided over by a judge pro tem, and that when Ms. Williams signed the Stipulation for Settlement, she was represented by counsel. A party's change of heart after agreeing to a settlement is not a valid ground for avoiding a contract. Nor is a party's subjective belief that she will not be bound by a signed settlement agreement. (See Mission Viejo Emergency Medical Associates v. Beta Healthcare Group (2011) 197 Cal.App.4th 1146, 1154-1155 ["In California, we adhere to the objective theory of contract law. Under that theory, '[i]t is the objective intent, as evidenced by the words of the contract, rather than the subjective intent of one of the parties, that controls interpretation.'"].) Contrary to Plaintiffs' argument, Defendants are not seeking to have the Court "create material terms to the settlement," but only to enforce it according to its terms. Nor does the parties' failure to sign the formal settlement documentation that was later prepared present a bar to its enforcement. (Blix Street Records, Inc. v. Cassidy (2010) 191 Cal.App.4th 39, 48 ["When parties agree that an agreement be binding, the fact that a more formal agreement must be prepared and executed does not alter the validity of the agreement."].) Counsel agree to confer regarding withdrawal of lis pendens.

Written order is signed and filed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: J. Rios Merida; Reporter: Anthony Vaughn, CSR No. 6185, tonyvaughn@yahoo.com
